summarization.a: The figure demonstrates selective TiN etching using O₃/HF at 250°C. TiN (gray diamonds) shows linear thickness reduction while Al₂O₃, HfO₂, ZrO₂, SiO₂, and Si₃N₄ remain essentially unchanged over 400 cycles. This confirms high selectivity of the ALE process for TiN over common dielectrics and barrier materials.

a.question: Which materials show etching and which remain unchanged during O₃/HF exposure?
a.answer: Etched: TiN only. Unchanged: Al₂O₃, HfO₂, ZrO₂, SiO₂, and Si₃N₄.

a.question: Why are Al₂O₃, HfO₂, and ZrO₂ not etched by this process?
a.answer: These metal oxides form stable, non-volatile fluorides (AlF₃, HfF₄, ZrF₄) when exposed to HF. Unlike TiF₄ which sublimes at 284°C, these fluorides remain on the surface and cannot be removed thermally under ALE conditions.

a.question: Why doesn't dry HF etch SiO₂ in this process?
a.answer: Dry HF (without H₂O present) cannot etch SiO₂. The HF/SiO₂ reaction requires water as a catalyst for effective etching.

a.question: Why is selective etching important for semiconductor device fabrication?
a.answer: Selective etching allows precise removal of one material while preserving adjacent structures. For TiN, selective ALE enables controlled thinning or removal without damaging surrounding dielectrics like HfO₂ gate oxides or SiO₂ interlayer dielectrics.
